caveats: a: The available BLS occupation mix combines NAICS 4242 and 4246 and is not specific to plastics distribution.
a: The frozen compensation-to-receipts ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and is harmonized to an IPS basis; resin price swings can materially distort labor intensity.
rho: The evidence is occupational and operational rather than a measured five-year adoption rate for the industry.
rho: Smaller distributors may lack normalized product, application, and transaction data needed for reliable implementation.
e: The industry report provides establishments, not firms or LMM eligibility.

e: The frozen LMM count is an ESTIMATE using a broad distributor EBITDA margin; resin price cycles, inventory gains, and the mix of commodity versus specialty products can materially shift firms across the band.
s5: One strategic acquisition demonstrates transferability but not incidence.
s5: BizBuySell covers a broader and generally smaller wholesale population, and workforce age is not owner age or sale intent.
q: No source directly measures pass-through of AI-enabled savings in plastics distribution.
q: Resin price and inventory movements can swamp operating savings and make gross-margin attribution difficult.
d5: The industry association is an advocacy organization and its long-run measure is broader than merchant distribution.
d5: Resin volume includes captive use and direct producer sales; wholesaler service demand can diverge.
d5: Tariffs, feedstock prices, construction and automotive cycles, recycled-content rules, and single-use restrictions widen the range.
o: Operator need is higher for specialty and regulated applications than for truckload commodity resin.
o: A digital marketplace can preserve an accountable operator in a thinner form; this primitive is not a labor-share estimate.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Distributors buy materials on their own account and earn a gross spread while providing repeat availability, supplier access, credit, material and regulatory guidance, inventory buffering, cut-to-size or light fabrication, and delivery coordination. Revenue is predominantly product sales rather than separately billed service fees.
lens.excluded_activities: Resin and plastic-product manufacturers classified outside 424610; wholesale agents and brokers that do not take title; captive internal distribution; pure financial commodity trading; nontransferable owner-only books; shells; and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market merchant wholesalers of plastic resins, compounds, unsupported film and sheet, rod, tube, and other basic shapes that repeatedly serve external processors, fabricators, OEMs, contractors, and institutional customers through sourcing, inventory, technical selection, cutting or other light value-added preparation, and logistics.

a.bridge: BLS publishes occupation counts for the combined nondurable merchant-wholesale group containing NAICS 4242 and 4246, not a wage-weighted task inventory for 424610. That group is led by technical and nontechnical sales representatives, managers, laborers, stockers, customer-service representatives, truck drivers, and shipping/inventory clerks. I applied higher AI exposure to quoting, account research, product and compliance retrieval, routine customer service, order entry, purchasing, inventory analysis, and document work, and lower exposure to warehousing, cutting, driving, hands-on quality work, technical troubleshooting, and final negotiation. Including drug wholesalers in the source group may overstate regulated clerical work, while plastics distributors' technical sales intensity may raise or lower exposure depending on workflow structure.
caveats: The available BLS occupation mix combines NAICS 4242 and 4246 and is not specific to plastics distribution.
The frozen compensation-to-receipts ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and is harmonized to an IPS basis; resin price swings can materially distort labor intensity.
a.change_evidence: Obtain a wage-weighted occupation roster and two-week activity logs from at least fifteen resin and shapes distributors, separated into technical sales, inside sales, customer service, purchasing, pricing, warehouse, cutting/fabrication, traffic, finance, and management; document tasks already automated before coding residual exposure.
a.rationale: Sales, quoting, specification search, purchasing, customer service, and transaction administration are major wage-bearing functions and lend themselves to AI assistance. A material share of labor remains physical or depends on accountable engineering judgment, supplier relationships, credit, and complex exceptions, keeping exposure below a majority at the base.

d5.bridge: ACC reports 2025 resin sales and captive use 1.3 percent above 2024, while the Plastics Industry Association reports long-run real plastics-manufacturing shipments growing about 0.2 percent annually and forecasts a 0.5 percent decline in 2025 followed by 1.3 percent growth in 2026. These measure resin use and broad plastics shipments, not five-year constant-quality demand for 424610 distribution services. I convert the mix of stable volumes and modest long-run growth into a 3 percent five-year base; it may overstate distributor demand if producers sell direct and understate it if fragmented customers outsource more inventory and technical support.
caveats: The industry association is an advocacy organization and its long-run measure is broader than merchant distribution.
Resin volume includes captive use and direct producer sales; wholesaler service demand can diverge.
Tariffs, feedstock prices, construction and automotive cycles, recycled-content rules, and single-use restrictions widen the range.
d5.change_evidence: Obtain annual inflation-adjusted 424610 product-line sales and physical volume from the Economic Census and distributor panels, then forecast resin, sheet, film, rod, and tube volume by end market and sales channel through 2031, including virgin-to-recycled substitution and direct-sales share.
d5.rationale: Plastic materials serve diversified packaging, healthcare, construction, transportation, and industrial end markets. Current resin volumes are stable to modestly growing, and circular-economy requirements shift the product mix toward recycled and redesigned materials rather than eliminating distribution, supporting a near-flat to low-growth service outlook.

caveats: The industry report provides establishments, not firms or LMM eligibility.

The frozen LMM count is an ESTIMATE using a broad distributor EBITDA margin; resin price cycles, inventory gains, and the mix of commodity versus specialty products can materially shift firms across the band.
e.change_evidence: Match the 2,575 establishments reported for NAICS 424610 to firm parents using Census Business Register or commercial location data, then classify normalized EBITDA, external repeat revenue, title-taking, value-added services, management depth, and owner dependence; reconcile firm counts to the frozen 473 estimate.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition is a coherent merchant-distribution model, and industry examples show recurring inventory, material-selection, technical-support, cutting, and logistics services. Most true LMM operating firms should fit the lens, but pure traders, captive units, manufacturers misclassified by mixed activity, owner-only relationship books, and EBITDA-band errors reduce eligibility.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 424610 ($3,268,657,965) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 424610 ($49,940,494,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1137 (margins.json[42]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Retail (Distributors) EBITDA/Sales 11.37%); revenue/employee $1,513,440 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 424610); class <5: 933 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,235,293 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $367,853 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 304 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,949,487 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $790,157 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 236 firms, avg revenue/firm $16,999,593 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,932,854 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 237 firms, avg revenue/firm $35,334,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,017,476 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 79 firms, avg revenue/firm $122,343,570 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $13,910,464 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 93 firms, avg revenue/firm $244,710,946 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $27,823,635 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 473

caveats: Operator need is higher for specialty and regulated applications than for truckload commodity resin.
A digital marketplace can preserve an accountable operator in a thinner form; this primitive is not a labor-share estimate.
o.change_evidence: Measure producer-direct and self-service e-commerce shares using supplier and distributor transaction data; interview processors, OEM procurement teams, resin producers, insurers, and technical-service staff to identify who retains title, application recommendation, traceability, inventory, and product-claim liability in digital transactions.
o.rationale: Online catalogs and direct producer ordering can eliminate some basic brokerage and order-taking, but most year-five volume still needs an accountable distributor or equivalent operator for title, credit, inventory availability, lot and grade traceability, technical selection, cutting or fabrication, claims, and physical delivery. Circular materials add supplier qualification and documentation even as software handles more interaction.

caveats: No source directly measures pass-through of AI-enabled savings in plastics distribution.
Resin price and inventory movements can swamp operating savings and make gross-margin attribution difficult.
q.change_evidence: Analyze customer-SKU gross margins, quotes won and lost, rebates, freight, surcharges, renewal behavior, churn, and service-level commitments for five years around documented automation; separate commodity resin, specialty resin, and shapes customers and compare against non-adopting competitors.
q.rationale: Distributors earn product spreads rather than cost-plus reimbursement, so savings are initially internal. Commodity resin benchmarks, competitive quoting, supplier price moves, and e-commerce make price transparent and drive pass-through, while technical guidance, small-lot availability, cut-to-size service, credit, traceability, and rapid delivery support differentiation and retention.

rho.bridge: BLS expects AI and automation to constrain broad wholesale-sales and procurement work, not to measure 424610 implementation over five years. I translate this direction into adoption for distributor workflows and discount for ERP integration, proprietary supplier data, product-liability risk, credit controls, cybersecurity, and human approval of technical recommendations. The estimate may be low for scaled e-commerce distributors and high for small firms with fragmented product and customer data.
caveats: The evidence is occupational and operational rather than a measured five-year adoption rate for the industry.
Smaller distributors may lack normalized product, application, and transaction data needed for reliable implementation.
rho.change_evidence: Collect deployment and pre/post labor-hour evidence from plastics-distribution ERP, CRM, e-commerce, and inventory-optimization vendors and from distributors using AI; audit hallucination, wrong-grade, wrong-price, and delivery-error rates plus required human review by workflow.
rho.rationale: Product search, quote drafting, CRM updates, order capture, demand forecasting, purchasing suggestions, document extraction, customer status responses, and exception prioritization can be embedded in existing systems within five years. Human validation remains essential for safety-critical material selection, regulated applications, unusual processing problems, credit decisions, and pricing authority.

caveats: One strategic acquisition demonstrates transferability but not incidence.
BizBuySell covers a broader and generally smaller wholesale population, and workforce age is not owner age or sale intent.
s5.change_evidence: Build a 2021-2026 denominator-based control-transfer history for 424610 firms from PitchBook, Capital IQ, Axial, Plastics News, Modern Distribution Management, UCC records, supplier announcements, and state filings; interview IAPD members, lenders, and plastics M&A advisers to distinguish qualifying sales from branch purchases and internal reorganizations.
s5.rationale: The 2026 acquisition of Interstate Advanced Materials by Curbell confirms that a multi-location plastics distributor with its team and customer relationships can transfer, while broad wholesale data show an active business-sale market. Those observations lack an eligible-firm denominator. The base allows for consolidation and owner succession but discounts family or internal transfers, failed processes, supplier-consent issues, and acquisitions outside the LMM band.

principal_driver: Technical product selection and repeat inventory logistics create automatable information work around durable physical demand.
principal_weakness: Commodity price transparency and producer-direct channels limit benefit retention, while physical and engineering work constrains substitution.

caveats: The a and rho magnitudes are judgmental transformations of a combined 4242-and-4246 occupation mix and broad occupational AI direction; no cited source measures a wage-weighted 424610 task share or five-year implementation rate.

S5 verifies the reported establishment and shipment statistics, but establishments are not firms and broad plastics-manufacturing growth is not five-year constant-quality demand for merchant-distribution services.
The q estimate has no direct evidence on pass-through of AI-enabled savings; the cited sources support sector context and service differentiation, not the 0.52 base retention fraction.

The s5 estimate lacks an eligible-firm denominator: one strategic acquisition establishes transferability, BizBuySell covers a broader and generally smaller market, and workforce age does not measure owner succession or sale intent.
S7 is a single distributor's self-description and therefore supports workflow existence, not population prevalence or the numerical bases used for rho, e, q, and o.
S11 establishes public goals for circular plastics technologies and supply chains but does not measure wholesaler demand or operator necessity; the d5 and o implications remain extrapolations.

Commodity resin distribution and value-added sheet, rod, tube, and specialty-material distribution may differ materially in labor exposure, retention, and operator need despite the packet marking the lens non-heterogeneous; the packet partly acknowledges this in its ranges and caveats.

The stated principal weakness says commodity transparency limits benefit retention even though the base C factor is capped at 10; physical-work constraints, reflected in H, are the actual binding weakness in the base score.

sources_audited.note: The cited Census page and its linked 2022 NAICS manual define 424610 as merchant wholesale distribution of plastics materials and resins and unsupported film, sheet, sheeting, rod, tube, and other basic forms and shapes.

The May 2024 OEWS table for the disclosed combined 4242-and-4246 group lists the stated sales, management, material-moving, stocking, customer-service, driving, and shipping/inventory occupations. It is not 424610-specific or wage weighted, as the packet acknowledges.

BLS lists prospecting, needs analysis, product selection, price and availability responses, negotiation, contracts, orders, follow-up, reporting, and administrative work, and says online sales mostly complement face-to-face selling while AI and chatbots may limit employment growth.

BLS describes supplier evaluation, price analysis, negotiation, contracts, ordering, inventory and sales-record review, and states that some procurement tasks will continue to automate or use AI while complex supply-chain and negotiation work remains.

The report verifies 2,575 establishments, 40.4 thousand employees, and $62.011 billion of 2024 shipments for 424610, plus the cited broad plastics-manufacturing history and 2025-2026 forecast. It does not directly support LMM eligibility, AI-savings retention, or five-year demand for 424610 distribution services.

The March 2026 ACC table reports 2025 plastics production of 136.123 billion pounds, up 0.1%, sales and captive use of 138.130 billion pounds, up 1.3%, and thermoplastic sales and captive use up 1.6%.

QTM describes material selection, processing guidance, on-site technical support, quality control, and just-in-time shipment, supporting the existence of value-added distributor workflows. A single company's marketing page does not establish their prevalence or quantify adoption, eligibility, retention, or operator need.

Curbell's April 14, 2026 announcement documents its acquisition of Interstate Advanced Materials, nine locations, more than 150 employees, continued key leaders and team members, and continuity of customer contacts and operations.

BizBuySell reports wholesale/distribution businesses sold nationally from 2021 through 2025 and a five-year median revenue of $1,378,849. The population is broad and much smaller than the stated LMM lens, and the page provides no plastics-distributor denominator.

Census reports that the share of wholesale-trade employment at firms with at least one-quarter of workers over 55 rose from 14% in 2000 to over 40% in 2022. This is workforce composition, not owner age, succession intent, or control-transfer incidence.

DOE states a 2030 plastics-innovation strategy covering deconstruction, upcycling, recyclable-by-design materials, and scale and deployment in a domestic plastics supply chain. Preservation of 424610 demand, added documentation burden, and a high operator-required share are reasonable but unmeasured extrapolations.

sources.supports: Describes inventory and sales analysis, supplier identification, contracts, and ordering; says procurement tasks will continue to automate and may use AI while humans remain needed for complex supply chains and negotiation.
sources.title: Purchasing Managers, Buyers, and Purchasing Agents

sources.supports: Reports 2,575 establishments, 40,400 employees, and $62.011 billion of 2024 shipments for NAICS 424610; reports broad real plastics shipments growing about 0.2 percent annually during 2014-2024 and a 2025 decline followed by 1.3 percent projected growth in 2026.

sources.supports: Reports total plastics production of 136.123 billion pounds in 2025, up 0.1 percent, and sales plus captive use of 138.130 billion pounds, up 1.3 percent; thermoplastic sales and captive use rose 1.6 percent.

sources.supports: A U.S. resin distributor describes material selection, processing guidance, on-site technical support, quality control, and just-in-time shipments as part of its customer service, illustrating repeat value-added workflows.
sources.title: QTM Plastic Resin Distribution Services

sources.supports: Documents an April 2026 control acquisition of a nationwide plastics and composites distributor with nine locations and more than 150 employees, with key leaders and teams continuing after the transaction.
sources.title: Curbell Plastics Acquires Interstate Advanced Materials

sources.supports: Reports observed sales of wholesale and distribution businesses during 2021-2025, including annual median prices and a five-year median revenue of $1.379 million; the covered population is broader and smaller than this lens.

sources.supports: Reports that over 40 percent of wholesale-trade employment in 2022 was at firms where at least one-quarter of workers were over 55, up from 14 percent in 2000; this is workforce rather than owner evidence.
sources.title: Firms in Production Sectors and Northern States Have Some of the Highest Shares of Older Workers

sources.supports: Sets a 2030 strategy for plastic deconstruction, upcycling, recyclable-by-design materials, and scaled domestic circular supply chains, indicating product-mix and documentation change rather than elimination of plastic-material flows.
sources.title: U.S. Department of Energy Strategy for Plastics Innovation
